cards.p: Statement #1. What does it actually show?
cards.a: A company's financial performance over a period — a quarter or a year.
cards.b: It answers one question: did the business make a profit?
cards.c: Its bottom line, net income, becomes the starting point for both other statements.
cards.d: For a utility, 'performance' is shaped by the regulator — revenue is largely set by approved rates, not the open market.
cards.ex: Exelon, Q1 FY2026 top to bottom: Revenue $7,242M → Operating income $1,605M → Net income $919M. That bottom line then starts the cash flow statement and feeds retained earnings.

cards.p: The top line. When is it recorded?
cards.a: The value of goods or services sold, recognized when earned.
cards.b: It's the starting point — everything below is subtracted from it.
cards.c: Booked when earned, not when cash arrives. That gap is why a cash flow statement exists.
cards.d: Utility revenue is regulated rates × volumes plus pass-through cost recovery — not a price the company sets freely.
cards.ex: Duke Energy, Q1 FY2026 revenue = $9,178M — roughly regulated rate × volume + pass-through cost recovery, not a price set freely on the open market.

cards.p: Revenue minus what?
cards.a: Revenue minus the cost of goods or services sold (COGS).
cards.b: Shows how profitable the core product is before overhead.
cards.c: Gross margin feeds the profitability ratios in the Metrics unit.
cards.d: Utilities don't really report COGS or gross profit; their analog is margin after fuel and purchased power (pass-throughs).
cards.ex: Textbook: Revenue − COGS = $494.8M − $235.0M = $259.8M gross profit (52.5% margin). But for a utility this misleads — that 'COGS' is largely fuel and purchased power passed through at cost, not a marked-up product.

cards.p: Strip out financing and tax. What's left?
cards.a: Gross profit minus operating expenses like SG&A and R&D.
cards.b: Isolates how well the core business runs, regardless of financing or tax.
cards.c: EBIT drives interest coverage and launches free cash flow.
cards.d: For a regulated utility this is effectively what the allowed return on rate base produces — regulator-shaped, not market-shaped.
cards.ex: Operating margin = Operating income ÷ Revenue = $2,018M ÷ $8,397M = 24.0% (Southern, Q1 FY2026). For a regulated utility this is essentially what the allowed return on rate base produces.

cards.p: The bottom line. Where does it go next?
cards.a: What remains after interest, taxes, and all expenses — the profit.
cards.b: The single most-quoted earnings number.
cards.c: It flows two places: retained earnings on the balance sheet, and the top of the cash flow statement. The first link.
cards.d: A utility's net income is judged against the allowed return on equity set in its last rate case.
cards.ex: Net margin = Net income ÷ Revenue = $621M ÷ $5,091M = 12.2% (Dominion, Q1 FY2026). Judge a utility's net income against the allowed ROE set in its last rate case.

cards.p: Why can a profitable company go broke?
cards.a: Accrual accounting records revenue/expense when earned, not when cash moves.
cards.b: Profit is not the same as cash in the bank.
cards.c: This mismatch is the entire reason for the cash flow statement — Unit 3.
cards.d: Regulatory accounting adds a timing layer — costs can be deferred as regulatory assets and recovered in later periods.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024: net income $47.1M but operating cash flow $130.0M — cash far exceeds profit, mostly because non-cash depreciation is added back. Profit ≠ cash in the bank.

cards.p: Statement #2. A period or a moment?
cards.a: A snapshot of what a company owns and owes at a single point in time.
cards.b: It shows financial position and soundness, not performance.
cards.c: The income statement covers a span; this is one frozen instant — usually period-end.
cards.d: A utility's balance sheet is dominated by one thing: net utility plant — the rate base it earns a return on.
cards.ex: Southern, Q1 FY2026 (one frozen moment): Assets $157,031M — mostly net utility plant — funded by Liabilities $117,119M + Equity $39,912M. A snapshot, not a period.

cards.p: The rule that must always hold?
cards.a: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Always, by construction.
cards.b: Every transaction keeps it in balance — the heart of accounting.
cards.c: This is the 'balance sheet balances' tie. If it fails, the books are wrong.
cards.d: Utilities carry items others don't — regulatory assets and liabilities — that exist only because a regulator allows them.
cards.ex: Exelon, Q1 FY2026: Assets $117,545M = Liabilities $88,230M + Equity $29,315M. Check the work: $88,230M + $29,315M = $117,545M — it balances to the dollar. If the two sides don't tie, the books are wrong.

cards.p: What the company owns. Two flavors?
cards.a: Resources owned: current (cash, receivables, inventory) and long-term (PP&E, intangibles).
cards.b: Assets are the engine that generates revenue.
cards.c: Net PP&E here is what depreciation on the income statement slowly wears down.
cards.d: Net utility plant (PP&E) is the asset that matters most — it IS the rate base that drives utility earnings.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024 assets $1,794.5M — only $188.8M is current (cash, receivables); the rest is long-term net utility plant, the rate base. That huge base is why asset turnover is tiny: $494.8M revenue ÷ $1,794.5M assets = 0.28×.

cards.p: What the company owes. To whom?
cards.a: Obligations: payables, accrued costs, short- and long-term debt.
cards.b: They fund assets and reveal financing and risk.
cards.c: Debt here drives the interest expense back on the income statement.
cards.d: Utilities run heavy long-term debt by design; regulators build a cost of debt into approved rates.
cards.ex: Debt ÷ assets = $90,245M ÷ $198,048M = 45.6% of Duke's asset base is debt-funded (Q1 FY2026) — heavy long-term debt is by design; regulators build that cost of debt into approved rates.

cards.p: The owners' slice — and the first big link?
cards.a: Equity = assets minus liabilities. Retained earnings = cumulative profit kept in the business.
cards.b: The shareholders' claim and a record of reinvested profit.
cards.c: Each period's net income flows straight into retained earnings. Link #1, made concrete.
cards.d: Equity is half the capital structure regulators reason about — they set an allowed return on this equity base.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024 equity $512.5M. Each year's profit lands here: FY2024 net income $47.1M was added to retained earnings inside that balance. Return on that base: $47.1M ÷ $512.5M = 9.2% ROE — judged against the allowed ~9–10%.

cards.p: Current assets minus current liabilities. Why care?
cards.a: The short-term cash cushion to run day-to-day operations.
cards.b: Too little strains liquidity; too much is idle cash.
cards.c: Changes in working capital reappear on the cash flow statement — consuming or releasing cash.
cards.d: Utilities often run thin working capital and tight current ratios; short-term debt bridges the constant capex spend.
cards.ex: Working capital = Current assets − Current liabilities = $6,623M − $12,594M = −$5,971M (AEP, Q3 FY2024). Current ratio = 6,623 ÷ 12,594 = 0.53× — negative working capital is normal for a utility; short-term debt bridges the constant capex spend.

cards.p: Statement #3. What gap does it close?
cards.a: It tracks actual cash in and out over a period, in three buckets.
cards.b: It reconciles accrual profit to real cash — the test of solvency.
cards.c: It exists because net income isn't cash. It proves where the cash went.
cards.d: For utilities the cash story is stark: strong operating cash, enormous capex, and a financing gap to fill.
cards.ex: Dominion, Q1 FY2026 in three numbers: operating cash $882M, capex $1,462M — an $882M − $1,462M = −$580M gap that financing must fill. That's the utility cash story in miniature.

cards.p: Where does it start?
cards.a: Cash from the core business, starting from net income and adjusting for non-cash and working-capital items.
cards.b: Growing operating cash flow is the strongest sign of quality.
cards.c: It literally begins with net income from the income statement — link #2.
cards.d: Operating cash flow is large for utilities largely because depreciation on a giant plant base is huge.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024 CFO = $130.0M, built up from net income $47.1M plus large non-cash add-backs. CFO ÷ revenue = $130.0M ÷ $494.8M = 26.3%.

cards.p: A non-cash expense. Why add it back?
cards.a: Depreciation cut net income but moved no cash, so it's added back.
cards.b: Explains why cash flow often exceeds reported profit.
cards.c: Depreciation touches all three: expense on the IS, accumulated on the BS, added back here.
cards.d: For utilities this add-back is enormous — the plant base is so large it dwarfs most other line items.
cards.ex: Why Unitil's CFO ($130.0M) is ~2.8× its net income ($47.1M): depreciation on the huge plant base is added back — it cut reported profit but moved no cash.

cards.p: Why does rising inventory hurt cash?
cards.a: More receivables or inventory consume cash; more payables release it.
cards.b: A 'profitable' quarter can still burn cash if working capital balloons.
cards.c: These swings come straight from the balance sheet's current accounts.
cards.d: Less of a swing factor for utilities; the dominant cash story is relentless capex, not working capital.
cards.ex: For Unitil the swing is small: working capital is only −$39.9M ($188.8M − $228.7M), while capex runs $175.0M a year. Capex, not working capital, drives the cash story.

cards.p: The other two buckets?
cards.a: Investing = capex and acquisitions. Financing = debt, equity, dividends.
cards.b: They show how the company invests and funds itself.
cards.c: Capex builds the PP&E on the balance sheet; debt and dividends move liabilities and equity.
cards.d: This is the utility's whole identity — massive investing outflows (capex) funded by financing (debt + equity).
cards.ex: Southern, Q1 FY2026: investing outflow ≈ capex $2,944M, while CFO was $1,226M — the ~$1,718M gap is filled by financing (new debt and equity) to fund rate-base growth.

cards.p: The number that closes the loop?
cards.a: Beginning cash plus the net change across all three buckets.
cards.b: The final cash position for the period.
cards.c: It must equal the cash line on the balance sheet. The last tie — loop closed.
cards.d: Utilities hold very little cash; capital flows almost straight from financing into plant. (Unitil ended a year near $6M.)
cards.ex: Duke held $2,140M cash at Q1 FY2026 — cash ratio = $2,140M ÷ $20,334M current liabilities = 0.11×. Utilities hold little cash; capital flows almost straight from financing into plant.

cards.p: Income statement to balance sheet?
cards.a: Each period's net income increases retained earnings within equity.
cards.b: It's how performance accumulates into position over time.
cards.c: Profit doesn't vanish at year-end — it's banked into owners' equity.
cards.d: Retained earnings help fund rate-base growth, reducing how much new equity a utility must issue.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024: $47.1M net income flows into the $512.5M equity balance as retained earnings — banked profit that helps fund rate-base growth, so less new equity must be issued.

cards.p: Income statement to cash flow?
cards.a: Net income is the first line of the operating section.
cards.b: Cash flow is built by reconciling that profit to actual cash.
cards.c: Same number, two destinations — equity and the top of cash flow.
cards.d: True for utilities too — though their cash flow is defined more by the depreciation add-back than the net income line.
cards.ex: Unitil's cash flow starts at the same $47.1M net income, then builds to CFO $130.0M via the big depreciation add-back. Same number, two destinations: equity and the top of the cash flow statement.

cards.p: Cash flow back to balance sheet?
cards.a: Ending cash on the cash flow statement equals the cash asset on the balance sheet.
cards.b: It proves the statements are internally consistent.
cards.c: If these don't match, something is mis-stated. A hard tie.
cards.d: Holds exactly for utilities — though their cash line is tiny next to the plant base.
cards.ex: Unitil's cash-flow ending cash ($6.3M) must equal the cash asset on the balance sheet ($6.3M) — a hard tie. If those two differ, something is mis-stated.

cards.p: How do you sanity-check any company's books?
cards.a: Balance sheet balances; ending cash ties; net income ties to the cash flow's first line.
cards.b: If all three hold, the statements are consistent — your first data-quality test.
cards.c: These are exactly the green/red checks in the Three-Statement Reader tool.
cards.d: These are how you sanity-check a utility filing before quoting its rate base, ROE, or capex to a client.
cards.ex: Unitil check: (1) Assets $1,794.5M = Liabilities $1,282.0M + Equity $512.5M ✓; (2) ending cash ties at $6.3M ✓; (3) net income $47.1M starts the cash flow ✓. All three hold — the filing is internally consistent.

cards.p: Why do you need all three statements?
cards.a: Income statement = profitable; balance sheet = sound; cash flow = solvent.
cards.b: Each covers a blind spot of the others — one alone misleads.
cards.c: A company can look profitable yet be insolvent. Only all three tell the truth.
cards.d: Utilities are reliably profitable yet rarely self-funding — solvency leans on continuous access to capital markets.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024: profitable (net income $47.1M) and sound (equity $512.5M) — yet FCF = $130.0M − $175.0M = −$45.0M. Not self-funding; solvency leans on capital-market access.

cards.p: Gross, operating, net — the ladder?
cards.a: Profit at each level divided by revenue.
cards.b: Shows where money is made or lost down the statement.
cards.c: Margin compression is quantifiable pain — and a reason to modernize for real-time visibility.
cards.d: Use margin after pass-through costs for utilities — raw revenue includes fuel and purchased power they don't mark up.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024 margin ladder (each ÷ revenue $494.8M): gross 52.5% ($259.8M), operating 18.3% ($90.6M), net 9.5% ($47.1M). For a utility, read margin after pass-through fuel/power costs.

cards.p: Can they pay the short-term bills?
cards.a: Current ratio (current assets / current liabilities) and the stricter quick ratio.
cards.b: They flag short-term solvency risk.
cards.c: Tight liquidity makes working-capital release a compelling ERP ROI story.
cards.d: Don't be alarmed by a sub-1 current ratio at a utility — it's structural, backed by predictable regulated cash flows.
cards.ex: NextEra current ratio = Current assets ÷ Current liabilities = $13,858M ÷ $25,573M = 0.54× (Q1 FY2026). Cash ratio: $1,998M ÷ $25,573M = 0.08×. Sub-1 is structural for a utility, not a red flag.

cards.p: How much debt, and can they cover it?
cards.a: Debt-to-equity sizes leverage; interest coverage (EBIT / interest) tests the cushion.
cards.b: Over-levered firms are fragile when rates or earnings move.
cards.c: Leverage context shapes how you frame TCO and deal financing.
cards.d: Utilities run roughly 50/50 debt/equity or higher by design; stable cash flows justify it. (Unitil sits near 1.5× D/E.)
cards.ex: Southern debt-to-equity = Total debt ÷ Equity = $74,522M ÷ $39,912M = 1.87× (Q1 FY2026) — high by design. Interest coverage = EBIT ÷ interest = $2,018M ÷ $778M = 2.59×, so earnings cover interest about 2.6×.

cards.p: How well is capital used?
cards.a: Net income over equity, assets, or invested capital.
cards.b: Returns above the cost of capital create value; below it, destroy it.
cards.c: Returns are boardroom language — anchor your value case to them.
cards.d: A utility's ROE only means something next to its allowed ROE from the rate case — that's the real benchmark.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024: ROE = $47.1M ÷ $512.5M = 9.2%; ROA = $47.1M ÷ $1,794.5M = 2.6% (low — the asset base is huge). Compare that 9.2% ROE to the allowed ROE set in the rate case.

cards.p: DSO + DIO − DPO. What does it reveal?
cards.a: The days cash is tied up between paying suppliers and collecting from customers.
cards.b: A shorter cycle self-funds growth and cuts financing needs.
cards.c: Connecting order-to-cash and procure-to-pay in one ERP tightens every leg.
cards.d: Less central for utilities (no inventory-heavy cycle); the capex funding cycle matters far more.
cards.ex: Less central for a utility — no inventory cycle. The funding cycle dominates instead: CFO $130.0M vs capex $175.0M leaves a $45.0M gap to finance every year.

cards.p: The number boards reward?
cards.a: Operating cash flow minus capital expenditures.
cards.b: It funds dividends, buybacks, debt paydown, and M&A.
cards.c: Better EPM forecasting makes FCF predictable — and predictability earns CFO trust.
cards.d: Utilities often run persistently negative FCF from heavy capex by design — that's healthy here, not a warning.
cards.ex: Unitil FCF = CFO − capex = $130.0M − $175.0M = −$45.0M. Negative by design; CFO ÷ capex = 0.74× means operations cover ~74% of the build, and debt/equity funds the rest.

cards.p: Your best opening question?
cards.a: The period-end process of finalizing the books. Best-in-class is ~3–5 days.
cards.b: A slow close delays decisions and signals weak controls.
cards.c: Ask 'what's your current days-to-close?' — it surfaces pain in one question.
cards.d: Utilities add regulatory reporting on top of the close — FERC and state-commission filings with their own deadlines.
cards.ex: Illustration: best-in-class close ≈ 3–5 business days; a laggard takes 10–15+. Every extra day delays the numbers leaders decide on. Ask 'what's your days-to-close?' — a slow answer surfaces the pain in one question.

cards.p: The spreadsheet graveyard?
cards.a: Matching GL balances to subledgers and supporting detail.
cards.b: Unreconciled accounts are audit risk and potential misstatement.
cards.c: Automated, continuous reconciliation replaces the month-end marathon.
cards.d: Utilities also reconcile regulatory assets and liabilities — accounts that don't exist in other industries.
cards.ex: Illustration: a mid-size utility may reconcile thousands of GL accounts every month. If even 5% still live in spreadsheets, that's hundreds of manual, audit-risk reconciliations — exactly what continuous automation removes.

cards.p: Detail vs. summary — why both?
cards.a: Subledgers hold transaction detail; the GL holds summarized balances.
cards.b: Tracing a balance to its source is what makes books auditable.
cards.c: Fusion subledger accounting keeps an automatic audit trail.
cards.d: Utility fixed-asset subledgers are massive (every pole, transformer, meter) — plant accounting is a discipline of its own.
cards.ex: Illustration: one GL line — 'Net utility plant $1.5B' — can sit atop a fixed-asset subledger of millions of individual assets (poles, transformers, meters). Tracing that $1.5B back to its source detail is what makes the books auditable.

cards.p: Why is this personal for the CFO?
cards.a: Controls over financial reporting; SoD ensures no one controls a whole transaction.
cards.b: Executives personally certify controls — real legal liability.
cards.c: Continuous controls monitoring cuts audit cost and material-weakness risk.
cards.d: Same SOX rules, plus regulatory audits — utilities answer to commissions as well as the SEC.
cards.ex: SoD illustration: the person who sets up a new vendor should not also approve that vendor's payments — one person controlling a whole transaction is the exact gap SOX targets. Executives personally certify these controls.

cards.p: The coding spine. Why does a messy one hurt?
cards.a: The structured list of accounts classifying every transaction.
cards.b: A sprawling CoA breaks reporting, consolidation, comparability.
cards.c: Oracle EDM rationalizes and governs the CoA so reporting rolls up cleanly.
cards.d: Utilities map to the FERC Uniform System of Accounts — a prescribed chart layered onto their own.
cards.ex: Illustration: a sprawling chart of accounts with 40,000 accounts breaks reporting and comparability; a rationalized ~5,000-account structure rolls up cleanly. Utilities must also map every account to the FERC Uniform System of Accounts.

cards.p: Many entities, one set of books?
cards.a: Combining subsidiaries and removing intercompany transactions.
cards.b: Errors overstate consolidated revenue and profit.
cards.c: Fusion consolidation automates eliminations — vital for multi-entity groups.
cards.d: Holding companies like Exelon, AEP and Southern — many regulated subs under one parent — make consolidation and intercompany a real challenge.
cards.ex: Illustration: if two Exelon subsidiaries bill each other $50M, consolidated revenue must eliminate that $50M — otherwise the group overstates revenue by $50M. Multiply across many subs and the eliminations get large fast.

cards.p: The heart of a regulated utility?
cards.a: The net plant value on which a regulator lets the utility earn a return.
cards.b: Utilities grow earnings mainly by growing rate base — investing in plant.
cards.c: Plant accounting accuracy is everything — why utilities run PowerPlan alongside or within Oracle Fusion.
cards.d: Your whole book lives here — Exelon, Southern, Duke, NextEra, AES, CMS all grow earnings by growing rate base.
cards.ex: Unitil's ~$1.5B+ net utility plant IS its rate base. Earnings ≈ allowed ROE × rate base, so growing the plant grows earnings. Roughly: 9.5% allowed × rate base ≈ the return it's permitted to earn.

cards.p: Who sets a utility's profit?
cards.a: The return on equity a regulator permits in a rate case.
cards.b: Actual ROE is judged against this allowed number — the scorecard.
cards.c: Compare the company's real ROE to its allowed ROE to gauge regulatory health.
cards.d: Multi-state holders like Exelon (ComEd, PECO, BGE, Pepco) litigate a separate allowed ROE in each state — usually ~9–10%.
cards.ex: Actual vs allowed: Unitil ROE = $47.1M ÷ $512.5M = 9.2%, against a typical allowed ~9–10%. Earning right at allowed signals a healthy regulatory relationship.

cards.p: Why is CFO/capex below 1× normal here?
cards.a: Utilities spend more on plant than operations generate, year after year.
cards.b: They fund the gap with debt and equity, grow rate base, earn a return on it.
cards.c: The entire utility model — exactly what Unitil's CFO/capex of ~0.74× showed.
cards.d: Unitil spends ~35% of revenue on capex with CFO/capex near 0.74× — the textbook regulated-utility profile.
cards.ex: Unitil FY2024: capex $175.0M ÷ revenue $494.8M = 35% capex intensity; CFO ÷ capex = $130.0M ÷ $175.0M = 0.74×. Below 1× — operations don't cover the build. Textbook utility.

cards.p: Balance-sheet items unique to utilities?
cards.a: Costs or benefits deferred to future periods because regulators allow recovery or require return.
cards.b: They smooth earnings and exist only because of regulation.
cards.c: They complicate the close and reporting — a clear modernization driver.
cards.d: These deferrals — large at Dominion, Duke and Southern — are exactly why utilities need strong ERP and regulatory accounting.
cards.ex: Illustration: a utility spends $100M on storm restoration but the regulator lets it recover the cost over 5 years. It parks a $100M regulatory asset and bills ~$20M/year — an account that exists only because a regulator allows it.

cards.p: Why isn't fuel a profit lever?
cards.a: Fuel, purchased power, and water supply are recovered from customers, not marked up.
cards.b: It's why utility 'gross margin' strips out these pass-throughs.
cards.c: Revenue optics can mislead — focus on margin after pass-throughs.
cards.d: Fuel and purchased-power recovery — think Georgia Power or PSEG — is why headline revenue can swing without touching profit.
cards.ex: Why utility gross margin misleads: Unitil's $235.0M 'COGS' is largely fuel and purchased power passed through at cost. Strip it out and judge margin after pass-throughs — not the headline 52.5%.

cards.p: What makes water capital even heavier?
cards.a: Very long-lived assets (pipes, treatment) and sustained rate-base investment.
cards.b: Aging infrastructure means decades of capex and rate-base growth.
cards.c: Long asset lives stress fixed-asset and regulatory accounting — fertile ERP ground.
cards.d: Water names in your book — H2O America (ex-SJW), Connecticut Water, Maine Water, Aquarion, Veolia, United Water — carry the longest asset lives.
cards.ex: Illustration: a water main can be depreciated over 50–100 years, versus ~30–40 for power plant. Longer asset lives mean decades of sustained rate-base investment — and heavy fixed-asset and regulatory accounting.

cards.p: What kind of company is Berkshire?
cards.a: A holding company owning dozens of businesses plus a large equity portfolio.
cards.b: Its results are the sum of wildly different subsidiaries.
cards.c: Each sub often runs its own systems — a consolidation and standardization opportunity.
cards.d: Berkshire spans utilities (BHE), distribution (McLane, TTI/Mouser) and brands (Duracell, Acme Brick, Brooks, Justin) — many on your account list.
cards.ex: Illustration: Berkshire's result is the SUM of dozens of businesses — insurance + BNSF rail + BHE utilities + manufacturing + a huge equity portfolio. No single margin describes it; you read each segment on its own.

cards.p: How does Berkshire run its subsidiaries?
cards.a: Extreme decentralization — subs operate autonomously with a tiny HQ.
cards.b: Capital allocation is centralized; operations are not.
cards.c: Fragmented finance systems = the CAO's consolidation headache and your opening.
cards.d: BHE's utilities run autonomously — as do Duracell, Acme Brick and Brooks — under a tiny HQ, so finance systems stay fragmented.
cards.ex: Illustration: a headquarters of only a few dozen people oversees hundreds of thousands of employees across the subsidiaries — so each sub keeps its own finance systems. That fragmentation is precisely the consolidation and standardization opening.

cards.p: Berkshire's secret financing engine?
cards.a: Premiums held before claims are paid — money Berkshire invests meanwhile.
cards.b: Low- or no-cost financing that fuels investments.
cards.c: Float is a balance-sheet liability that behaves like free capital — a vivid working-capital lesson.
cards.d: Berkshire's float helps fund capital-heavy businesses like BHE's utility build-out — cheap capital meeting rate base.
cards.ex: Illustration: if insurers hold $100 of premiums before paying claims, Berkshire invests that $100 in the meantime — low- or no-cost financing. Float behaves like free capital: a balance-sheet liability that quietly funds investments.

cards.p: Buffett's preferred profit measure?
cards.a: Reported earnings plus non-cash charges, minus maintenance capex.
cards.b: Approximates true distributable cash — a cousin of free cash flow.
cards.c: It reframes the income statement through a cash-and-capex lens. Speak it to finance leaders.
cards.d: For a capital-heavy utility like BHE, maintenance capex is large, so owner earnings sit well below reported earnings.
cards.ex: Owner earnings ≈ Net income + non-cash charges − maintenance capex. Illustration: profit $10 + depreciation $4 − maintenance capex $5 = $9 owner earnings. For capital-heavy BHE, large maintenance capex pulls owner earnings below reported profit.

cards.p: Berkshire's core skill?
cards.a: Deciding where the cash from subsidiaries is reinvested across the empire.
cards.b: The single biggest driver of long-run shareholder value.
cards.c: Reliable subsidiary-level data is the raw material for good capital allocation.
cards.d: Berkshire pours capital into BHE precisely because regulated utilities offer steady, rate-base-driven returns.
cards.ex: Illustration: cash thrown off by subs (say insurance + rail) gets redeployed where returns are best — often into BHE's regulated utility build-out, because rate base delivers steady returns. That reinvestment decision is the core skill.

cards.p: Where do energy and your deals fit?
cards.a: BNSF rail, Berkshire Hathaway Energy (utilities!), McLane and TTI distribution, Duracell, Acme Brick, Brooks, Justin, and more.
cards.b: BHE links Berkshire back to the utilities unit; McLane is a real Oracle case.
cards.c: Standardizing finance across these subs is exactly the story you're already selling.
cards.d: BHE is the utilities link; McLane, TTI/Mouser, Duracell, CORT and Acme Brick are all on your account list.
cards.ex: Illustration: BHE links Berkshire straight back to the utilities unit, and McLane (distribution) is a real Oracle ERP case. Standardizing finance across such different subs is exactly the story you're already selling.

cards.p: What is the Fusion suite, in one line?
cards.a: Oracle's complete cloud (SaaS) business suite — ERP, EPM, HCM, SCM and CX — built on one common data and security model.
cards.b: One vendor, one suite, one source of truth: finance, HR, supply chain and customer data stop living in silos.
cards.c: Open with 'how many separate systems run your finance, HR and supply chain today?' — fragmentation is the opening.
cards.d: The pillars: ERP (finance), EPM (planning & close), HCM (people), SCM (supply chain), CX (front office).

cards.p: Why does 'one suite' actually matter?
cards.a: Every Fusion app shares a single data model and security model, so modules connect without custom integration.
cards.b: Finance, HR and operations reconcile automatically — no integration tax, no data that disagrees between systems.
cards.c: If their finance and HR numbers never quite match, that mismatch is the pain you solve.
cards.d: Contrast with bolting together separate ERP, HR and CRM products from different vendors.

cards.p: How does Oracle deliver new capability?
cards.a: Fusion ships new features every quarter (e.g. the 25x/26x releases) — no disruptive 're-implementation' upgrades.
cards.b: Customers get continuous innovation and stay current automatically, instead of falling years behind on-prem.
cards.c: Ask when they last upgraded their on-prem ERP — multi-year upgrade pain reframes 'cloud' as relief.
cards.d: Same quarterly cadence underpins ERP, HCM, SCM, EPM and CX together.

cards.p: What's Oracle's AI story in Fusion?
cards.a: AI is embedded across the suite, plus new Fusion Agentic Applications — objective-based workspaces where AI agents collaborate to reach an outcome.
cards.b: It automates routine finance, HR and supply-chain work — a concrete productivity and cost message.
cards.c: Ask where teams are stuck in manual, repetitive work — that's the agentic-AI use case.
cards.d: Recent releases added agentic apps across ERP, HCM, SCM and CX.

cards.p: Where does Fusion fit for legacy Oracle shops?
cards.a: Fusion Cloud is the modern SaaS successor to on-prem Oracle E-Business Suite, PeopleSoft and JD Edwards.
cards.b: It gives legacy customers a clear, supported modernization path off aging on-prem systems.
cards.c: 'Running EBS or PeopleSoft?' instantly frames the cloud-migration conversation.
cards.d: Utilities often run decades-old on-prem finance — a prime Fusion ERP modernization target.

cards.p: What does Cloud ERP cover?
cards.a: Cloud financial management plus procurement, project management, and risk management — the finance system of record.
cards.b: It runs the close, reporting and controls every CFO is judged on. This is the core of most Oracle deals.
cards.c: Lead with 'how many days does your financial close take?' — a slow close surfaces pain fast.
cards.d: Utilities layer FERC and state-commission reporting on top of the standard close.

cards.p: What's inside the financials core?
cards.a: General Ledger, Payables, Receivables, Fixed Assets and Cash Management — the books and sub-ledgers.
cards.b: It's where the three statements you teach actually get produced and tied out.
cards.c: Connect it back: this is the engine behind the income statement, balance sheet and cash flow.
cards.d: Fixed Assets matters enormously to a utility — net plant IS the rate base.

cards.p: Why does procurement ride with ERP?
cards.a: Sourcing, purchasing, supplier management and payables in one source-to-pay flow.
cards.b: Tighter spend control and supplier risk visibility — direct margin and working-capital impact.
cards.c: Tie to working capital: faster, controlled procure-to-pay releases cash.
cards.d: Capital-heavy utilities buy enormous volumes of plant and materials — procurement scale is real.

cards.p: How does ERP make controls easier?
cards.a: Advanced Financial Controls and Access Controls automate segregation-of-duties and continuous monitoring.
cards.b: Executives personally certify controls under SOX — automation cuts audit cost and material-weakness risk.
cards.c: This is the SOX & segregation-of-duties card from the Close unit, made into a product.
cards.d: Utilities answer to both the SEC and state commissions — double the audit scrutiny.

cards.p: What does agentic AI do for finance?
cards.a: AI agents automate invoice processing, anomaly detection, and account reconciliation inside the close.
cards.b: It compresses days-to-close and reduces manual error — a measurable ROI story.
cards.c: Pair with the reconciliation card: the 'spreadsheet graveyard' is exactly what AI eliminates.
cards.d: Reconciling regulatory assets/liabilities is utility-specific manual work AI can streamline.

cards.p: What is Oracle Utilities CCS?
cards.a: A cloud customer information system (CIS) — customer care, service orders, metering and billing in one SaaS product for utilities of all sizes.
cards.b: It's the utility's revenue and customer-engagement engine — billing accuracy and customer experience live here.
cards.c: Ask 'how old is your CIS / billing system?' — most utilities run aging on-prem CIS.
cards.d: Part of Oracle Energy and Water (formerly Oracle Utilities).

cards.p: What makes CCS more than a billing system?
cards.a: CCS combines Customer Care & Billing, Meter Data Management, and Operational Device Management as a single solution.
cards.b: Three systems become one — far fewer integrations to build, run and audit.
cards.c: If they run separate CIS, meter-data and device systems, the consolidation story is compelling.
cards.d: Pre-built integration to head-end and AMI (smart meter) systems is included.

cards.p: Why is utility billing uniquely hard?
cards.a: CCS handles complex regulated rate structures, multiple service types, and pass-through cost recovery.
cards.b: Regulated rates and fuel/purchased-power recovery make billing logic far more complex than a normal invoice.
cards.c: Tie to the pass-through-costs card: this is where regulated revenue actually gets calculated.
cards.d: Supports one-to-many service types (electric, gas, water) on a single platform.

cards.p: What about the customer side?
cards.a: Service order management plus multi-channel customer communication (web, mobile, contact center).
cards.b: Customer satisfaction and regulator-watched service metrics depend on this workflow.
cards.c: Ask about their self-service and contact-center experience — friction there is a CX opening.
cards.d: Captures data from multiple sources for a single view of the customer.

cards.p: How does CCS relate to legacy products?
cards.a: CCS is the SaaS evolution of on-prem Customer Care & Billing (CC&B) and Customer to Meter (C2M).
cards.b: It gives existing CC&B/C2M customers a cloud path without abandoning the platform they know.
cards.c: 'On CC&B on-premises?' immediately frames CCS as the cloud move.
cards.d: Many of your utility accounts still run on-prem CC&B today.

cards.p: What is Oracle Utilities NMS?
cards.a: Real-time outage and distribution management for the electric grid — combining outage (OMS) and distribution (DMS) management.
cards.b: Grid reliability and outage response are board-level and regulator-watched — NMS runs them.
cards.c: Ask 'how do you manage outages and restoration today?' — reliability is always top-of-mind.
cards.d: Pairs with CCS so customer outage calls and the grid view stay in sync.

cards.p: What does the OMS half do?
cards.a: Predicts and locates outages, manages field crews, and orchestrates restoration.
cards.b: It directly drives SAIDI/SAIFI reliability metrics that regulators and customers judge utilities on.
cards.c: Reliability scores tie to rate cases — better outage response supports the regulated return story.
cards.d: Outage calls flow from CCS customers into NMS for a unified response.

cards.p: What does the DMS half do?
cards.a: Monitors and controls the distribution network and supports distributed energy resources (DER).
cards.b: Grid modernization and rooftop-solar/EV growth make active distribution control essential.
cards.c: Tie to rate base: grid-modernization capex is exactly the rate-base growth utilities earn on.
cards.d: Supports the shift to a two-way grid as renewables and DER scale.

cards.p: What problem does MDM solve?
cards.a: Collects, validates (VEE), and stores massive volumes of smart-meter / AMI interval data.
cards.b: Accurate validated meter data is the foundation of correct billing and grid analytics.
cards.c: Ask about their AMI rollout — meter data volume explodes and needs industrial-scale management.
cards.d: Feeds validated reads straight into CCS billing; also available within CCS.

cards.p: Why does the integration matter?
cards.a: Pre-built integration links the customer/metering system (CCS) to the grid system (NMS).
cards.b: One connected view of customer and grid — outages, service orders and metering stay consistent.
cards.c: The integration story is the differentiator: customer + grid + meter data on one Oracle stack.
cards.d: Documented in Oracle's Customer-to-Meter to Network Management System integration guides.

cards.p: What does Cloud HCM cover?
cards.a: A complete cloud suite for the whole employee lifecycle — core HR, payroll, talent, and workforce management.
cards.b: People are a utility's largest cost after capital; one HR system replaces a pile of point tools.
cards.c: Ask 'how many separate systems touch an employee from hire to retire?'
cards.d: An aging utility workforce and wave of retirements make this urgent.

cards.p: What's the system-of-record layer?
cards.a: A single global HR record plus Oracle Payroll for org, pay and compliance.
cards.b: One source of truth for the workforce, instead of reconciling HR and payroll across systems.
cards.c: Probe multi-entity, multi-state payroll complexity.
cards.d: Utilities span regulated subsidiaries and unionized workforces with complex pay rules.

cards.p: How does Oracle help win the talent war?
cards.a: Recruiting, Onboarding, Performance, Compensation, Career Development and Succession in one module.
cards.b: Attracting and keeping scarce skilled labor (linemen, engineers) is existential for utilities.
cards.c: 'What's your time-to-fill for safety-critical roles?'
cards.d: Succession planning matters as experienced field crews retire.

cards.p: Where does labor cost get controlled?
cards.a: Time, labor, scheduling and absence management.
cards.b: Directly controls overtime, labor cost and compliance.
cards.c: Overtime and scheduling pain in field operations is a fast opening.
cards.d: Storm and outage response demands flexible, rapid crew scheduling.

cards.p: What's Oracle's employee-experience play?
cards.a: Oracle ME — Journeys, Touchpoints, Celebrate and Communicate — guided, GenAI-assisted employee experiences.
cards.b: Engagement and a modern experience retain talent and lift productivity.
cards.c: 'How do new hires get onboarded today?'
cards.d: Deskless field workers need a mobile-first experience.

cards.p: What does Cloud SCM span?
cards.a: A connected suite across planning, procurement, inventory, manufacturing, logistics and order management.
cards.b: Resilient supply chains; for capital projects, materials availability drives the schedule.
cards.c: 'How do you manage materials for capital projects?'
cards.d: Utilities procure huge volumes of poles, transformers, cable and meters.

cards.p: What does planning solve?
cards.a: Demand and supply planning with machine learning to balance inventory, capacity and materials.
cards.b: Avoids both stockouts and excess by predicting what's needed where.
cards.c: Long-lead-time equipment is a planning nightmare — surface it.
cards.d: Grid build-out hinges on planning scarce long-lead components like transformers.

cards.p: What does inventory give you?
cards.a: Visibility into every item and movement, automated replenishment, and serial/lot tracking.
cards.b: Cuts carrying cost and stockouts — right materials, right place, right time.
cards.c: Spare-parts and MRO inventory across service territories is a common pain.
cards.d: Utilities stock critical spares across many yards and substations.

cards.p: How does SCM procurement help?
cards.a: Sourcing, supplier management, purchasing and payables with built-in AI.
cards.b: Enforces compliant spend, best-supplier selection and margin protection.
cards.c: Ties to ERP — supplier risk and maverick spend are openings.
cards.d: Massive regulated capital-procurement programs make this high-stakes.

cards.p: What about assets and movement?
cards.a: Supply Chain Execution (costing, manufacturing, maintenance, quality) plus Logistics (warehouse, transportation, global trade).
cards.b: Executes precisely from material handling to delivery and asset upkeep.
cards.c: Asset maintenance (EAM) for plant is the utility-relevant angle.
cards.d: Maintaining a vast distributed asset base is core to utility operations.

cards.p: What is EPM, above ERP?
cards.a: The planning, consolidation & close, reconciliation and reporting layer that sits above the ERP ledger.
cards.b: It's the CFO's forecasting and close engine — it directly operationalizes the finance you teach.
cards.c: 'How long does your forecast and close cycle take?'
cards.d: EPM's modules ARE the Close & Controls unit, turned into products.

cards.p: What does EPM Planning do?
cards.a: Connected planning with dashboards, predictive planning and machine learning.
cards.b: Faster, more accurate forecasts make cash flow predictable.
cards.c: Tie to the free-cash-flow card — predictability earns CFO trust.
cards.d: Utilities plan multi-year rate-base capital programs here.

cards.p: Which finance card is this?
cards.a: Streamlines consolidation, intercompany eliminations and the financial close.
cards.b: It's the 'consolidation & eliminations' card as a shipping product.
cards.c: Multi-entity holding companies (Exelon, AEP, Southern) feel this pain most.
cards.d: Layers regulatory reporting on top of GAAP consolidation.

cards.p: What kills the spreadsheet graveyard?
cards.a: Reconciliation compliance, period management and automated transaction matching.
cards.b: It's the 'account reconciliation' card automated end to end.
cards.c: 'How many accounts are reconciled in spreadsheets each month?'
cards.d: Reconciling regulatory assets and liabilities is utility-specific work.

cards.p: What governs data and the story?
cards.a: Enterprise Data Management governs master data like the chart of accounts; Narrative Reporting authors and publishes financial & regulatory reports.
cards.b: A clean CoA plus collaborative reporting — the 'chart of accounts' card as a product.
cards.c: A sprawling, messy CoA breaks consolidation and comparability.
cards.d: Maps to the FERC Uniform System of Accounts utilities must follow.

cards.p: What is CX, beyond CRM?
cards.a: A connected front-office suite — Sales, Service, Marketing and CPQ — that goes past traditional CRM.
cards.b: Customer experience is increasingly regulator-watched for utilities, and central for any seller.
cards.c: 'How do you handle customer interactions across channels?'
cards.d: Covers residential and C&I customer experience, distinct from CCS billing.

cards.p: What's the revenue engine?
cards.a: Sales, quoting, order management, billing and renewals on one platform with better forecast accuracy.
cards.b: Predictable revenue and a reliable buying experience.
cards.c: B2B and C&I sales motions are the entry point.
cards.d: Utility commercial & industrial key-account management lives here.

cards.p: How does service work?
cards.a: B2C Service, Field Service and Intelligent Advisor for lasting customer relationships.
cards.b: Efficient, consistent service across channels and the field.
cards.c: Field-service scheduling and dispatch is a tangible pain.
cards.d: Utilities run large field-service organizations.

cards.p: What's in the marketing stack?
cards.a: Eloqua (B2B automation), Responsys (B2C campaigns) and Unity Customer Data Platform.
cards.b: Personalized engagement built on unified customer data.
cards.c: Program campaigns (energy efficiency, demand response) need targeting.
cards.d: Utility customer programs depend on segmented, timely communication.

cards.p: What does CPQ fix?
cards.a: Configure, Price, Quote — configure the right products and produce accurate quotes; connects to Commerce, ERP and Order Management.
cards.b: Faster, accurate quotes shorten the sales cycle and protect margin.
cards.c: Complex product and pricing scenarios are the trigger.
cards.d: Complex C&I rate and product quoting benefits most.

cards.p: What is OIC in one line?
cards.a: One service that unifies application integration, process automation, visual app building and integration analytics.
cards.b: It connects SaaS and on-prem so the Fusion suite and legacy systems actually talk.
cards.c: 'How do your cloud and on-prem systems talk today?'
cards.d: Connects utility CCS/NMS to Fusion ERP and HCM.

cards.p: Why is OIC fast to build with?
cards.a: Prebuilt adapters plus Recipes (templates) and Accelerators connect apps roughly six times faster.
cards.b: Less custom code and a shorter time-to-value.
cards.c: A big integration backlog or high integration cost is the opening.
cards.d: Utilities juggle many systems — CIS, GIS, NMS, ERP — that all need connecting.

cards.p: What does the process side do?
cards.a: Structured and unstructured process automation plus case management.
cards.b: Automates cross-application workflows end to end.
cards.c: Manual handoffs between systems signal the need.
cards.d: Streamlines utility back-office processes across departments.

cards.p: How does OIC stay real-time?
cards.a: Subscribe to Fusion business events to trigger downstream actions; supports real-time, batch and data integration.
cards.b: Event-driven integration keeps systems in sync as things happen.
cards.c: Data-sync latency between systems is a concrete complaint.
cards.d: Keeps meter, customer and grid data consistent across the stack.

cards.p: What about monitoring?
cards.a: Technical and business end-to-end data-flow visibility and integration insight.
cards.b: See and quantify transactions in business terms, not just logs.
cards.c: 'Can you trace one transaction across all your systems?'
cards.d: Supports audit and compliance traceability utilities require.

cards.p: What is Oracle University?
cards.a: Oracle's training and certification platform (MyLearn) — role-based learning paths, hands-on labs and live expert sessions.
cards.b: Adoption and value realization depend on trained users; it de-risks the whole deal.
cards.c: 'How will your team get trained and certified before go-live?'
cards.d: Offers a mix of free and paid subscription content.

cards.p: What's the subscription model?
cards.a: Unlimited digital training: the Cloud Applications subscription covers ERP/EPM/HCM/SCM/CX; the Technology subscription covers OCI, Database, Java, MySQL and APEX.
cards.b: Cost-effective, organization-wide enablement that scales with the customer.
cards.c: Frame it as the enablement-budget conversation.
cards.d: Each subscription includes certification exam attempts.

cards.p: Why do certifications matter?
cards.a: Role-based certifications validate skills across Fusion apps and OCI.
cards.b: Certified teams implement faster and make fewer costly mistakes.
cards.c: Tie to staffing and partner (SI) capability.
cards.d: Certifications are a credibility signal for staff and implementation partners.

cards.p: How do learners practice?
cards.a: Training on Demand plus hands-on labs in real Oracle environments.
cards.b: Teams practice safely in a sandbox before go-live.
cards.c: Position it as go-live risk reduction.
cards.d: Materials are machine-translated into 20+ languages.

cards.p: How do you prove ROI on training?
cards.a: Admin dashboards track subscriber activation and consumption at macro and micro levels.
cards.b: Measure enablement ROI and keep adoption on track across the org.
cards.c: 'How will you measure user adoption after go-live?'
cards.d: Ties training to realized value — a renewal and expansion story.

solutions.tl: How to use it in a pitch

cards.p: How did a 1.4M-customer utility roll out time-of-use rates?
cards.a: Evergy — serving ~1.4 million customers across Kansas and Missouri — worked with Oracle Opower on behavioral energy insights, proactive alerts and digital self-service to support its move to default time-of-use (TOU) rates.
cards.b: Adoption was strong: about 30% of customers pre-enrolled in a TOU rate and ~80% enrolled through digital self-service — a smooth, customer-centric transition.
cards.c: Lead with this when a customer-experience or rate-change project comes up — proof that digital engagement drives rate-program adoption.
cards.d: Oracle Opower (Utilities Customer Experience).
cards.ex: ~1.4M customers · 30% pre-enrolled in TOU · 80% enrolled via digital self-service.

cards.p: A water utility replaced its whole billing platform — how fast?
cards.a: JXN Water (Jackson, Mississippi) needed to migrate 65,000 customers to a brand-new system, deploying Oracle Utilities Customer Cloud Service and Advanced Meter Service as its meter-to-cash platform.
cards.b: The cloud, open-platform approach let JXN stand up a reliable meter-to-cash system in an eight-month timeframe — fast for a full CIS replacement.
cards.c: Perfect for a utility on an aging CIS worried about migration risk — cloud CCS goes live in months, not years.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Customer Cloud Service (CCS) + Advanced Meter Service.
cards.ex: 65,000 customers migrated to a new meter-to-cash platform in ~8 months.

cards.p: How does a multi-state utility giant standardize its systems?
cards.a: Exelon — parent of ComEd, PECO, BGE and Pepco, among others — is deploying Oracle Energy and Water across its multi-state enterprise, and was recognized in Oracle's 2024 Energy and Water Awards.
cards.b: One platform across many regulated subsidiaries cuts fragmentation and standardizes customer and grid operations at enterprise scale.
cards.c: Exelon is on your account list — lead with a peer story: a top US utility holding company is standardizing on Oracle.
cards.d: Oracle Energy and Water (enterprise-wide).
cards.ex: Rollout spans Exelon's multi-state operating companies (ComEd, PECO, BGE, Pepco, and more).

cards.p: What's the proof utility energy programs actually deliver?
cards.a: Oracle Opower runs AI-driven energy-efficiency and customer-engagement programs for utilities across North America — personalized insights, alerts and behavioral load-shaping.
cards.b: The aggregate impact is the ultimate proof point: tens of millions of households engaged, billions in customer bill savings, and measurable energy savings that support grid resiliency.
cards.c: When a prospect doubts customer programs deliver ROI, cite the portfolio-level numbers — a proven, at-scale platform.
cards.d: Oracle Opower (Utilities Customer Experience / Energy Efficiency).
cards.ex: ~45M North American households benefiting; ~44 TWh saved ≈ $4.3B in customer bill savings since 2009.

cards.p: What did South Carolina's largest public power provider modernize?
cards.a: Santee Cooper, South Carolina's largest public power provider, implemented Oracle Utilities Customer Cloud Service to modernize customer operations for its electric account holders.
cards.b: Its nightly billing now runs on average about three hours faster, and communication overhead between modules dropped sharply.
cards.c: Great for a public-power or co-op prospect worried about batch-billing windows and system sprawl.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Customer Cloud Service (CCS).
cards.ex: Nightly billing ~3 hours faster; module-to-module overhead sharply reduced.

cards.p: How did a 108-year-old municipal utility enter retail competition?
cards.a: After more than 108 years as Lubbock's municipal electric provider, LP&L transitioned to a retail-competition business model backed by Oracle's utility customer and Advanced Metering Infrastructure (AMI) technology.
cards.b: Oracle's platform gave LP&L the customer and metering foundation to offer competitive retail electricity options for the first time.
cards.c: Use it for a municipal utility facing market or regulatory change — Oracle supports a whole new business model, not just a billing swap.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities customer platform + Advanced Meter Infrastructure (AMI).
cards.ex: Enabled a shift to retail electric competition after 108 years as a municipal provider.

cards.p: How does a city utility manage assets and storm reporting?
cards.a: The City of St. Petersburg uses Oracle Utilities Work and Asset Cloud Service (WACS) to simplify operations and maximize asset performance across its utility services.
cards.b: It manages 500,000+ assets, tracks nearly 50,000 timesheet entries, and generates FEMA-compliant reports — critical for disaster cost recovery.
cards.c: Strong for water or municipal prospects with heavy asset bases and storm/FEMA reporting needs.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Work and Asset Cloud Service (WACS).
cards.ex: 500,000+ assets managed; ~50,000 timesheet entries; FEMA-compliant reporting.

cards.p: How does a 5.6M-customer utility unify customer experience?
cards.a: AEP — powering 5.6 million customers across 11 states — consolidated nine different vendor solutions onto Oracle Opower for a single customer-experience platform, and is migrating to Oracle Utilities Customer Cloud Service for meter-to-cash.
cards.b: One secure data model now spans energy efficiency, digital self-service, high-bill alerts, rate comparisons, and equity/affordability outreach.
cards.c: AEP is on your account list — a flagship peer story: consolidating nine vendors onto one Oracle platform.
cards.d: Oracle Opower + Utilities Customer Cloud Service (CCS) + Customer Experience.
cards.ex: 5.6M customers, 11 states; nine vendor solutions consolidated onto one Opower platform.

cards.p: How did an Oregon utility lift customer satisfaction?
cards.a: Portland General Electric used Oracle solutions to deliver more personalized, proactive customer service and heightened engagement — earning recognition in Oracle's 2024 Energy and Water Awards.
cards.b: It exceeded customer expectations through efficient issue resolution and proactive communication.
cards.c: Pair it with Evergy for a customer-experience transformation story.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Customer Experience.
cards.ex: Recognized in Oracle's 2024 Energy and Water Awards for customer engagement.

cards.p: What did the largest US gas utility achieve with energy reports?
cards.a: Southern California Gas (SoCalGas) sends Opower Home Energy Reports to customers each month to help them understand and reduce natural gas use.
cards.b: The program cut natural gas use by 10 million therms since 2013 and saved customers more than $11 million on their bills.
cards.c: A perfect proof point for a gas utility weighing an energy-efficiency or behavioral program.
cards.d: Oracle Opower (Home Energy Reports).
cards.ex: 10M therms of gas saved since 2013 ≈ $11M+ in customer bill savings.

cards.p: How does a big-city utility get value from smart meters?
cards.a: Con Edison uses Oracle's Advanced Metering solutions to help customers understand the information and value delivered by their smart meters.
cards.b: Turning raw AMI data into clear customer insight drives engagement and smart-meter ROI.
cards.c: Good for a utility mid-AMI-rollout wondering how to make meter data useful to customers.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Advanced Metering.
cards.ex: Smart-meter data turned into customer-facing insight at metro scale.

cards.p: How is a community utility tackling decarbonization?
cards.a: Sacramento Municipal Utility District (SMUD), a community-owned electric utility, is partnering with Oracle Energy and Water on a customer-centric decarbonization solution — earning a 2023 Oracle Sustainability Award.
cards.b: It pairs clean-energy goals with a strong customer experience instead of treating them as separate tracks.
cards.c: Lead with SMUD when sustainability or decarbonization is on the customer's agenda.
cards.d: Oracle Energy and Water (customer decarbonization).
cards.ex: 2023 Oracle Sustainability Award for customer-centric decarbonization.

cards.p: How did a major utility make energy reports actually get read?
cards.a: National Grid piloted its first-ever video energy reports on Oracle Utilities Opower — emailing personalized videos to 33,000 Upstate New York customers — and ran a multichannel heat-pump adoption campaign on the same platform.
cards.b: The video reports earned 12× the click-through rate of standard digital energy reports, and 100,000+ customers saw the heat-pump content.
cards.c: Use it when engagement or program adoption (electrification, heat pumps) is the goal — richer content drives far higher response.
cards.d: Oracle Utilities Opower (video energy reports + energy efficiency).
cards.ex: 33,000 customers in the video pilot; 12× the click-through of standard reports; 100,000+ heat-pump program participants.

cards.p: Which platform did Xcel pick for customer engagement?
cards.a: Xcel Energy selected the AI-enabled Oracle Opower customer-engagement platform to support its energy programs, strengthen customer experiences, and improve grid resiliency.
cards.b: One AI platform ties energy programs, customer experience, and grid resiliency together instead of running them as separate efforts.
cards.c: A recent, marquee multi-state utility win — good for a 'why Opower' shortlist conversation.
cards.d: Oracle Opower customer-engagement platform (AI-enabled).
cards.ex: Selected Oracle Opower to power energy programs, CX, and grid resiliency (announced Dec 2025).

cards.p: Who goes first on a new Opower capability?
cards.a: Puget Sound Energy (PSE) in Washington is among the first providers to roll out the new Bill Advisor solution on the Oracle Opower platform.
cards.b: Bill Advisor proactively helps customers understand and manage their bills — cutting confusion and costly high-bill calls.
cards.c: Position PSE as an early-adopter proof point when a prospect worries about being first on new features.
cards.d: Oracle Opower (Bill Advisor).
cards.ex: Among the first utilities live on Opower's new Bill Advisor solution.

cards.p: How does an Australian network operator modernize?
cards.a: Essential Energy, an Australian electricity distributor, was recognized at Oracle's Customer Edge Summit for using modern digital technology to simplify operations, transform customer experience, and maximize asset performance.
cards.b: It shows the Oracle Energy and Water story extends well beyond North America to large international network operators.
cards.c: Use it to prove global scale and relevance for a multinational or non-US prospect.
cards.d: Oracle Energy and Water.
cards.ex: Recognized at Oracle Customer Edge for operations, CX, and asset-performance gains.

cards.p: How does a global logistics leader modernize finance?
cards.a: FedEx moved core enterprise functions to the cloud with Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP, EPM, SCM and Analytics — making finance as innovative as its customer-facing operations.
cards.b: With real-time data, FedEx responds to business changes about 2× faster and better supports both organic and acquired growth.
cards.c: A marquee cross-industry name for the 'one connected suite' story — finance, planning and supply chain on Oracle.
cards.d: Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP, EPM, SCM & Analytics.
cards.ex: Responds to change ~2× faster with real-time enterprise data.

cards.p: What happens when a tech firm consolidates finance on Oracle?
cards.a: Dropbox adopted Oracle Cloud to modernize and automate its finance operations.
cards.b: It cut transaction processing costs by ~80% and eliminated manual effort — freeing finance for higher-value work.
cards.c: Use the 80% figure for any 'automate the close / cut processing cost' conversation.
cards.d: Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP.
cards.ex: ~80% reduction in transaction processing costs.

cards.p: Why unify HR and finance on one platform?
cards.a: Staffing leader TrueBlue unified HR and finance on Oracle Fusion Cloud (HCM + ERP), giving employees and managers direct access to data.
cards.b: Productivity rose in both the back office and customer-facing roles once people could reach the data they needed — a truly connected enterprise.
cards.c: The go-to story for the HCM + ERP 'one data model' pitch — people and finance data in one place.
cards.d: Oracle Fusion Cloud HCM + ERP.
cards.ex: Productivity gains in both back-office and customer-facing roles after unifying HR + finance.

cards.p: How does a diversified conglomerate run on one suite?
cards.a: Al Jomaih Holding, a large diversified group, implemented Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP, SCM, HCM and EPM together, with Oracle Customer Success Services supporting deployment and adoption.
cards.b: It gained better reporting visibility, a stronger employee experience, and faster, data-driven decisions across its business units.
cards.c: A strong analog for a Berkshire-style multi-business group standardizing finance across subsidiaries.
cards.d: Oracle Fusion Cloud ERP, SCM, HCM & EPM (full suite).
cards.ex: Full Fusion suite live across multiple business units; faster data-driven decisions.

cards.p: How did a 40-year AS400 distributor modernize?
cards.a: TTI is a world-leading authorized distributor of electronic components. Its 40-year-old, heavily customized AS400 system was slowing business growth and its financial processes.
cards.b: Leadership modernized finance (starting with the GL), then carried the momentum into procurement — lowering TCO, cutting manual work, and speeding the financial close and time to market.
cards.c: Won over SAP on functionality and future roadmap; success at sister company Mouser (since 2014) plus a complete end-to-end platform sealed it.
cards.d: Oracle ERP Cloud + Procurement Cloud.
cards.ex: Retired AS400/COBOL dependency; automated manual processes; built-in AI now in use at TTI.

cards.p: What happens when a build-first shop moves to SaaS?
cards.a: Mouser is a global authorized distributor of semiconductors and electronic components. Its custom in-house ERP was hard to change and slowed the business.
cards.b: Since 2014 Mouser has grown its Oracle footprint year over year — from financials and procurement to HR modernization and customer engagement — cutting silos and manual work.
cards.c: Won on functionality vs SAP and in-house development; pre-built configurations beat costly custom builds, with an attractive AI roadmap.
cards.d: Oracle ERP, Analytics, Risk; SCM (Supply Planning, Execution, Order Mgmt, Transportation); HCM; EPM; Eloqua.
cards.ex: End-to-end suite across every business unit; less custom code, faster time to market.

cards.p: How do you turn a licensing problem into an expansion?
cards.a: CTB manufactures and supplies systems that help feed a growing population, running 14 brands across 46 facilities. It had been over-utilizing its Oracle subscription and C-suite confidence was wavering, with an RFP looming.
cards.b: Oracle resolved the overutilization by switching the licensing metric (Hosted Named User to Hosted Employee), unlocking an organization-wide Fusion rollout at a fraction of the cost — with no disruptive changes.
cards.c: Won on customer relationship and TCO; an 8-month engagement and deep grasp of the license challenge headed off an RFP to SAP.
cards.d: Oracle ERP, SCM (incl. CPQ), HCM, EPM.
cards.ex: Overutilization and managed-service cost removed; full-organization Fusion rollout unlocked.

cards.p: Can you automate a payroll no one else could?
cards.a: Forest River makes RVs, trailers, boats, buses and trucks across 100+ divisions. Its complex hourly/piecework/bonus payroll ran on manual Excel and had exposed it to time-and-labor lawsuits.
cards.b: Oracle digitized analog employee data and automated the complex processes other vendors couldn't — accelerating legal response and reducing liability, live in about six months.
cards.c: Won on a superior solution and strong executive rapport, replacing ADP and TruePay where competitors said it couldn't be done.
cards.d: Oracle HCM Core, Time & Labor, Payroll.
cards.ex: ~$300k/year in software savings; faster legal response; reduced liability exposure.

cards.p: How does a $6B manufacturer prepare for more M&A?
cards.a: Shaw is a $6B carpet manufacturer with 22,000+ employees, wholly owned by Berkshire Hathaway. Acquisitions had piled up legacy ERP and logistics, manual integrations and finance pain its systems couldn't handle.
cards.b: A single platform gave Shaw a source of financial truth (Accounting Hub), efficiency and cost control across the board, and readiness for further acquisitions.
cards.c: Won on relationship and functionality over Microsoft Dynamics; proven success at sister company Clayton Homes built the trust.
cards.d: Oracle ERP (Inventory, Procurement, Accounting Hub, Order Mgmt, OIC), EPM & EDM, SCM (Demand, Supply Planning, Product Mgmt), AI Data Platform.
cards.ex: Cost controls across the board; lower inventories and COGS; reduced IT headcount and legacy infrastructure.

cards.p: What replaces a cobbled-together EPM?
cards.a: Pilot is the largest network of travel centers in North America (fuel, food, truck maintenance). Its Infor/Lawson EPM was cobbled together, couldn't handle its international arm or complex driver-pay rules, and leaned on manual Excel budgeting.
cards.b: Oracle EPM delivered a single system of truth for driver-based planning — reducing information silos, improving planning efficiency, and substantially lowering TCO.
cards.c: Won on a comprehensive best-of-suite solution over OneStream; strong references from other Berkshire companies on Oracle EPM were decisive.
cards.d: Oracle EPM (with EDM).
cards.ex: Single source for driver-based planning; less manual Excel; substantially lower TCO.

cards.p: How does a vertically integrated builder scale on one platform?
cards.a: Clayton Homes is a $14B/year vertically integrated home builder, manufacturer, lender and retailer (~23,000 employees), wholly owned by Berkshire Hathaway. Legacy systems blocked enterprise-data insight as operations grew.
cards.b: A single, scalable platform gave Clayton one source of truth across very different business units and a strategic technology partnership within Berkshire.
cards.c: Won on relationship and functionality over Microsoft Dynamics, SAP and legacy AS400 via a consultative, business-value approach.
cards.d: Oracle ERP, EPM, Financial Accounting Hub, SCM, OTM, FDI, CPQ, OIC, OPERA, Oracle University.
cards.ex: Move-in time cut from 140 to 60 days; faster market reaction; economies of scale in purchasing.

cards.p: How do you standardize the close across divisions?
cards.a: PCC is a world leader in structural investment castings and forged components for aircraft engines and gas turbines. Legacy Hyperion, Excel reporting and fragmented divisional systems made consolidation, FX and intercompany eliminations painful.
cards.b: Oracle unified EPM across corporate and divisions — improving close, FX/eliminations/cash-flow insight and driver-based planning — with ERP addressing GL and reporting complexity.
cards.c: Won by replacing Hyperion, EBS and multiple Trintech/Blackline reconciliation tools with one global approach; an enterprise contract enabled expansion without new cycles.
cards.d: Oracle EPM (EPBCS, FCCS, ARCS, Narrative Reporting) + ERP (Accounting & Procurement).
cards.ex: Replaced 1 Trintech + 6 Blackline instances with one global ARCS; standardized consolidation and close.

cards.p: How do you modernize HR in a decentralized firm?
cards.a: HomeServices of America (HSoA), a Berkshire Hathaway affiliate, is one of the largest US residential real-estate brokerage and homeownership-services providers. It needed an HCM solution for its inefficiencies that fit a decentralized structure.
cards.b: A single HCM platform let HSoA own and manage the application with a support model that fit — lowering maintenance and support cost.
cards.c: Won on a superior solution and the CHRO relationship (she had implemented Fusion HCM at Ritchie Brothers) over UKG and Workday.
cards.d: Oracle HCM Core, Time & Labor, Payroll, Recruiting, Booster, Compensation, Help Desk, Talent & HCM Analytics.
cards.ex: Single HCM platform HSoA owns and manages; lower maintenance and support cost.
